title: Ekadashi Fasting
summary: The eleventh day of each lunar fortnight, observed as a fasting day in Vaishnava and Smarta traditions. Dedicated to Vishnu. Rules vary by strictness; below is the common observance.

steps.title: Know the timing
steps.instruction: Ekadashi falls twice a month on the 11th lunar day (tithi). The fast begins at sunrise on Ekadashi and breaks after sunrise on Dvadashi (the 12th), within a specific 'parana' window. Check a Hindu calendar (panchanga) for exact local timings.

steps.title: Choose your level
steps.instruction: Strictest: nirjala (no food, no water). Common: phalahara (fruits, milk, nuts, tubers — no grains, beans, or pulses). Lighter: one simple satvik meal without grains. Health-limited individuals should not attempt nirjala without guidance.

steps.title: Avoid grains and pulses
steps.instruction: The defining rule across levels: no rice, wheat, barley, millet, or lentils on Ekadashi. Permitted: fruit, milk, yogurt, potato, sweet potato, sago (sabudana), water chestnut flour (singhara), buckwheat (kuttu), rock salt (sendha namak).

steps.title: Spend the day in devotion
steps.instruction: Read the Vishnu Sahasranama, chant 'Om Namo Narayanaya' or 'Om Namo Bhagavate Vasudevaya', do extra japa, stay awake part of the night if possible (the Ekadashi jagarana).

steps.title: Break the fast correctly (parana)
steps.instruction: Break the fast on Dvadashi morning within the prescribed parana window (shown in the panchanga). Traditional first food is a small amount of cooked grain — often rice with tulsi water.

variants.label: Nirjala Ekadashi (once a year)
variants.instruction: The Jyeshtha-shukla Ekadashi (Bhima Ekadashi) is observed without water, said to equal all other Ekadashis combined. Only attempt if healthy.

sources.quote: Yoga is not for one who eats too much, nor for one who fasts too much...
